schools.summary: The blunt denial — 'not of men, neither by man, but by Jesus Christ' — answers the Judaizers' charge that Paul, not of the twelve, was no genuine apostle; the title in its strictest sense (Ellicott); God 'who raised him from the dead' the very Christ who commissioned him on the Damascus road.
Luther: Paul's arguments advance his ministry and discredit the false apostles; the divine call the ground of his authority.

themes: not of men, neither by man

schools.summary: 'All the brethren with me' (the small company of travelling companions) join the greeting; 'the churches of Galatia' marks a circular letter to several congregations, showing how widely the error spread; the absence of Paul's usual commendation reflecting the gravity of their defection.
Calvin and Luther: joining a body of brethren lends corporate weight to his witness against the false teachers.

'Grace and peace' Paul's habitual yet never perfunctory salutation — grace the free favour of God, the fountain of blessing, peace its fruit; both jointly 'from God the Father and our Lord Jesus Christ,' an implicit testimony to Christ's deity.
Luther expounds grace and peace as the sum of the Christian's blessedness, the very gifts the false gospel of works would destroy.

themes: from the Father and the Son

schools.summary: The cardinal doctrine set early — Christ 'gave himself for our sins' (a free, atoning self-offering, 1 Cor 15:3) 'to deliver us from this present evil world' (its guilt, corruption, and bondage, not the creation) 'according to the will of God' (springing from the Father's gracious purpose, not our merit).
Luther: Paul has nothing in his mouth but Christ, every word breathing fervency; the gospel of grace set against legalism.

themes: deliver from this present evil world

schools.summary: A doxology (no verb in the Greek, like Rom 11:36; 1 Tim 1:17) springing directly from the redemption just named; Paul's habit of bursting into praise in the midst of argument, that all glory be given to God; the thanksgiving designed to awaken a fresh sense of the gift received (Calvin).

In place of his usual thanksgiving, astonished reproof — 'so soon removed' (present tense, 'in the act of falling away,' the word used of a deserter/turncoat), deserting 'him that called you into the grace of Christ' for 'another gospel' (the Judaizers' legalism).
Luther: even here Paul begins gently, in a fatherly way, marvelling rather than thundering, though the offence is grave.

'Which is not another' — the false teaching no gospel at all but 'a yoke of bondage, the abolition of grace'; not glad but heavy tidings (Wesley); 'some that trouble you' would 'pervert the gospel of Christ' by adding works of the law to faith — to mix law and grace in justification destroys the gospel.

The extreme supposition — even Paul himself or 'an angel from heaven' preaching another gospel — shows the gospel's truth derives not from the messenger but stands above all; 'let him be accursed' (anathema, cut off from Christ); no credential can validate a corrupted gospel, the message judging the messenger.

A deliberate, not impulsive, repetition — 'so say I now again' — committing himself without retreat; the shift from himself/angels (v8) to 'any man,' bringing the curse home to the Judaizers; the gospel 'ye have received' the fixed apostolic measure condemning whatever departs from it.

The vehement anathema refutes the charge of men-pleasing — a man so willing to curse even apostles and angels does not court human favour; the contrast with his former life (once a man-pleaser, 'now' seeking God's favour); 'if I yet pleased men, I should not be the servant of Christ' — the two incompatible.

The thesis of the self-defence — the gospel Paul preached 'is not after man' (not of human invention, standard, or taste; no mark of man's cunning); the restored 'brethren' as he turns to instruct; if the gospel is divine in origin, no man may correct it (Luther: the principal hinge, defended to ch2's end).

A double denial and a divine affirmation — not 'received' as a tradition from Peter or another, nor 'taught it' as a pupil (in Gamaliel's school), but 'by the revelation of Jesus Christ' (the risen Lord unveiling it on the Damascus road); shutting out the charge that his gospel was a second-hand, correctable version from Jerusalem.

themes: neither received nor taught of man
by the revelation of Jesus Christ

schools.summary: The proof from his former life — his notorious 'manner of life in the Jews' religion,' persecuting 'beyond measure' and 'wasting' the church (Acts 9:1; 22:4); a man so fiercely opposed could not have come by the gospel naturally — only divine intervention turned the enemy into a preacher (Calvin: proving the conversion divine).

His eminence — he 'advanced' (proekopton, blazing ahead) beyond his peers, a brilliant pupil under Gamaliel (Phil 3:4-6), 'more exceedingly zealous of the traditions of the fathers,' which drove his persecution; a foremost zealot with every human reason to remain so, whom only an act of God could turn.

All traced to God's free pleasure — 'when it pleased God' (eudokesen), the contrast to his former zeal; 'separated me from my mother's womb' (aphorisas, set apart before birth, like Jeremiah, Jer 1:5) while yet a persecutor; 'called me by his grace,' not for any merit — the chief of persecutors called by grace alone.

The purpose 'to reveal his Son in me' (both to Paul in the vision and within him by grace) 'that I might preach him among the heathen' (the disputed Gentile commission); the proof of independence 'immediately I conferred not with flesh and blood' — he sought the gospel and its authorization from no man, needing no human confirmation.

themes: conferred not with flesh and blood

schools.summary: He did not 'go up to Jerusalem to them which were apostles before me' (acknowledging the twelve, yet not deriving his commission from them) but 'into Arabia, and returned to Damascus' — a withdrawal far from the leaders; had he received his gospel from the twelve he would have gone straight to them; he sought no man's authority (Calvin).

The long delay — 'after three years' he had ministered for years before seeing a Jerusalem apostle; 'to see Peter' (historesai, to get acquainted, not be instructed), as an equal seeking fellowship; and only 'fifteen days,' too short to be schooled — his gospel already formed and preached before he ever met Peter.

On that visit he saw only Peter and 'James the Lord's brother' (who led the Jerusalem church, reckoned an apostle in the broad sense, called 'the Just'); his exposure to the apostolic circle slight — two men, briefly — far too little to be the source of his gospel.

sides.position: a 'brother' (kinsman/cousin) of Jesus, head of the Jerusalem church, called 'the Just,' reckoned an apostle in the broad sense
identified by some with James the son of Alphaeus among the twelve

schools.summary: A solemn oath — 'before God, I lie not' — interrupting the narrative to call the Searcher of hearts to witness; an oath lawful only on weighty occasions (Calvin), showing how crucial the point is; sworn to secure belief and stop the false apostles' mouths (Luther).

After the brief Jerusalem visit, Paul departed for 'Syria and Cilicia' (Antioch and Tarsus, Acts 9:30), far from Jerusalem — the Hellenists having sought to kill him (Acts 9:29); his formative years spent at a distance from the apostles, not in their tutelage, again showing his gospel did not depend on them.

'Unknown by face' to the Judean churches (distinct from Jerusalem) — they had never seen him, let alone instructed him; closing off the last human channel, for with the apostles his contact was brief and with the Judean believers non-existent; the obscurity heightening his slanderers' malice (Calvin).

The Judean churches knew him only by report ('they were hearing from time to time'), framed in their own wondering words — 'he which persecuted us now preacheth the faith which once he destroyed' (Acts 9:13; 1 Tim 1:13); the change so complete and divine it became the talk of the churches, beyond any human explanation.

The churches 'glorified God in me' (imperfect, kept on praising) for the grace shown in his conversion; confirming the argument — they ascribed his change to God, not a human teacher, owning he owed all to God; the right attitude of admiration, the praise resting not in the man but rising to God (Ellicott).
